participants.professional_persona: 전북 지역 사립대학교에서 사회복지학 박사과정을 밟고 있으며, BK21 혁신인재양성사업의 지원으로 지역사회 복지 정책 연구를 수행합니다. 매달 130만 원의 장학금을 받으며 박사 3년차입니다.
participants.skills_and_expertise: 양적·질적 연구 방법론에 정통하고, SPSS와 NVivo를 활용한 데이터 분석이 가능합니다. 지역 복지관과의 협력 프로젝트 경험이 있습니다.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: 졸업 후 보건복지부나 지방자치단체의 정책연구원이 되어 사회 취약계층을 위한 실질적인 복지 정책을 개발하고 싶습니다.

participants.name: 영어영문학
participants.professional_persona: 영어영문학 박사 과정생으로, BK21 교육연구단에서 포스트식민주의 문학 비교 연구를 진행합니다. 연구 장학금 외에 번역 프리랜서로도 활동하며 경제적 안정을 찾았습니다.
participants.skills_and_expertise: 문학 텍스트 비평, 담론 분석, 영어-한국어 전문 번역 및 문화 이론 적용에 강점이 있습니다.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: 대학 교수로 임용되어 포스트식민주의 이론과 한국 문학의 접점을 연구하는 학자가 되는 것이 목표입니다.

participants.name: 한양대학교 (dkjyw4)
participants.professional_persona: 한양대학교 기계공학부 정교수로, BK21 혁신인재양성사업 스마트팩토리 분야 교육연구팀을 책임지고 있습니다. 스마트 제조 시스템 연구로 산업체 전환이 활발합니다.
participants.skills_and_expertise: 생산공학 자동화 및 IoT 기반 스마트팩토리 설계 전문가로, 대기업 공동연구 경험이 많습니다. 연구비 관리 및 예산 집행에 노하우가 있습니다.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: 교육연구팀의 예산을 자율적으로 효율적으로 집행하여 연구성과를 극대화하고, 팀 내 대학원생 인권교육을 체계적으로 완료하여 건강한 연구문화를 조성하고자 합니다.

participants.skills_and_expertise: 텍스트 마이닝과 고문서 해독에 능하며, 한국 고전 문학의 디지털 아카이브 구축 프로젝트에 참여하고 있습니다.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: 대학 연구소에서 디지털 인문학 분야를 개척하며, 문화유산의 보존과 대중화에 기여하고 싶습니다.

participants.skills_and_expertise: 딥러닝 모델 설계, 의료 영상 처리, Python·TensorFlow 고급 활용, 모델 경량화 및 배포 경험이 풍부합니다.

initial_posts.narrative: As a doctoral student in English literature at Hanyang University, I see the BK21 program's inputs from the ground. The policy context states the total annual budget for 2022 is 408,080 million KRW for education research groups plus 52,896 million KRW for graduate innovation support, totaling about 461 billion KRW. However, the policy also says the total support budget for 577 education research groups in 2022 is 408,080 million KRW including carryover, and the graduate innovation support is separate. I interpret 'total_funding_allocated' as the combined amount for Hanyang University specifically, not the national total. The policy context does not give Hanyang's exact share, but from the KG data, Hanyang is one of 68 universities with multiple research groups. I estimate Hanyang receives roughly 5-10% of the total, given its size and number of groups. The national total for education research groups is 355,151 million KRW (from resource_logic), plus graduate innovation support of 52,896 million KRW, totaling 408,047 million KRW. I'll use 410,597 million KRW as the national total (from KG data). For Hanyang, I estimate about 5% of that, or 20,530 million KRW, but the prediction field asks for total funding allocated to Hanyang University BK21 program. I think the policy context implies the total is for the entire program, not per university. The field name says 'total_funding_allocated' without specifying per university, so I'll use the national total as the most straightforward interpretation. The graduate scholarship slots: with monthly stipends of 700K-1.3M KRW, and a total budget of 355,151 million KRW for education research groups, if half goes to stipends, that's 177,575 million KRW per year. Dividing by average annual stipend per student (say 12 million KRW) gives about 14,800 slots. But the policy says 12,600 graduate students per year in future talent training and 6,400 in innovative talent training, totaling 19,000. I'll use 19,000 as the number of slots funded. Postdoc positions: minimum salary 3M KRW/month, so 36M KRW/year. If 10% of the budget goes to postdocs, that's 35,515 million KRW, supporting about 986 positions. But the policy context mentions 205 innovative talent training groups and 200 future talent groups, each likely having 1-2 postdocs. I estimate 300 postdoc positions nationally for Hanyang's share, but again the field is likely national. I'll use 300 as a rough estimate for Hanyang, but the field might be national. Given the ambiguity, I'll commit to national-level estimates for consistency.
evidence: The policy context states the total annual budget for education research groups is 355,151 million KRW and graduate innovation support is 52,896 million KRW, summing to 408,047 million KRW, which I round to 410,597 million KRW as per KG data.
The policy mentions 12,600 graduate students per year in future talent training and 6,400 in innovative talent training, totaling 19,000, which I use for graduate_scholarship_slots.
Postdoc positions are less clearly specified, but with 577 education research groups and each likely having 1-2 postdocs, I estimate 300 positions for Hanyang University based on its share of groups.

initial_posts.narrative: As a doctoral student in social welfare at a private university in Jeonbuk, I see the BK21 activities from the perspective of a participant in the Innovative Talent Training project. The policy context states that funds are disbursed in two installments: 70% in March and 30% in September, totaling 410,597 million KRW nationally. For Hanyang University, which likely receives about 5% of the national budget, the total funds disbursed would be approximately 20.53 billion KRW. The evaluation panels are convened for annual, mid-term, and compliance meetings, so I estimate 3 formal panel meetings per year. Consulting sessions are offered to education research groups that request them; the policy context mentions 280 groups out of 578 received consulting, so I assume a similar proportion for Hanyang's groups. My estimates are grounded in my group's experience and the policy context's emphasis on employment rate improvement.

initial_posts.narrative: As a doctoral student in social welfare at a private university in Jeonbuk, I see the Impact phase through the lens of a humanities and social sciences participant. The sustained investment in graduate talent development over a decade, as seen in the BK21 program's funding of 950 graduate slots and 15 postdocs at Hanyang University, gradually builds a pipeline of skilled researchers who contribute to national innovation capacity. However, the impact on national S&T talent competitiveness is modest because the program's scale is small relative to the entire country, and the benefits are concentrated in a few universities. The systemic changes in research culture—such as improved integrity, collaboration, and interdisciplinarity—are expected as alumni progress in their careers, but these changes take time and are not fully realized within the 2021-2022 window. The dissemination of outstanding group achievements and operational know-how through the BK21 webzine and forums influences other institutions, but the effect on national rankings is gradual. I estimate South Korea's rank in the global S&T talent competitiveness index to be around 15, a slight improvement from previous years, reflecting the program's contribution but also the competitive global landscape. For high-impact publications per 1000 researchers, I estimate 12.5, based on the 1,900 publications produced by Hanyang's 950 graduate students and 15 postdocs, but only a small fraction (about 1-2%) become top 1% cited, and the denominator includes all national researchers, not just BK21 participants. This estimate is lower than engineering-focused participants might assume, because humanities and social sciences fields have lower citation impact and publication rates.

initial_posts.narrative: As a postdoctoral researcher in computer engineering at Hanyang University, I see the Impact phase as the culmination of a decade of sustained investment in graduate talent development through BK21. The prior phases show that Hanyang has 950 graduate slots and 15 postdocs, with 20.53 billion KRW disbursed, leading to 2,375 publications and 1,425 international conference presentations. The outcomes phase indicated a 78.9% employment rate and 21% international collaboration rate, reflecting strong industry ties and global research networks. Over a decade, this pipeline of skilled researchers will gradually improve national innovation capacity. The systemic changes in research culture—integrity, collaboration, interdisciplinarity—are already visible as alumni move into academic and industry positions, fostering a more collaborative and ethical research environment. The dissemination of outstanding group achievements through the BK21 webzine and forums spreads best practices across institutions, raising the overall S&T ecosystem. I estimate South Korea's rank in the global S&T talent competitiveness index will improve to 12, up from around 15 in recent years, driven by the cumulative effect of BK21 and similar programs. For high-impact publications per 1000 researchers, I estimate 18.5, based on the 2,375 publications from Hanyang's 950 graduate students and 15 postdocs, with about 2-3% becoming top 1% cited (given engineering fields have higher citation impact than humanities), and scaling to the national researcher pool of roughly 400,000 full-time equivalent researchers. This is higher than the humanities participants' estimate of 12.5 because engineering and medical fields produce more high-impact publications. I disagree with the other participants' estimate of 12.5 for high-impact publications; my experience in AI/medical imaging shows that top 1% publications are more common in STEM fields, and the national average should reflect this.

forward_pass.phase_summary: The group discussed how sustained BK21 investment builds a skilled researcher pipeline, gradually improving national S&T talent competitiveness and high-impact publication rates, though effects are modest due to the program's limited scale and concentration in a few universities. Consensus emerged that the program's impact on national rankings is gradual, with systemic changes in research culture taking time to materialize. Disagreement centered on the magnitude of impact: humanities-focused participants estimated a national S&T talent competitiveness rank of 15 and high-impact publications per 1000 researchers at 12.5, citing lower citation rates in their fields, while engineering-focused participants estimated a rank of 12 and publications at 18.5, emphasizing higher STEM output. Predictions for the competitiveness rank ranged from 12 to 15, and for high-impact publications from 12.5 to 18.5, reflecting disciplinary differences in research output and citation impact.
